title: Setting up a meat rabbit colony
subtitle: Housing, breeding, and growing on a small colony of meat rabbits from purchase of stock to first slaughter.
excerpt: Meat rabbits are one of the most efficient converters of homegrown or foraged feed into table protein. A colony of six does with one buck can produce thirty to forty table rabbits a year on a modest footprint. This pattern covers the housing and bedding setup, sourcing stock, the first mating, and the grow-on schedule from birth to slaughter weight.
sourceNotes:
RSPCA, Welfare standards for rabbits (2019). https://www.rspca.org.uk
British Rabbit Council, breed and husbandry guides. https://www.thebrc.org

content.text: A small colony needs housing that is draught-free, vermin-proof, and easy to clean. Individual suspended wire cages are the simplest system: each
has her own space, which prevents fighting and makes monitoring straightforward. The
is housed separately except during mating.

Each adult cage should be at least 90 cm x 60 cm x 45 cm (length x depth x height). Wire floors 1.2 cm x 1.2 cm mesh allow droppings to fall through and should be checked regularly for sore hocks, a pressure sore that develops where rabbits rest on unsuitable wire. Provide a rest mat of solid material in each cage. Site the cages inside a shed or under a covered lean-to: rabbits tolerate cold better than heat and do not cope well with rain, wind, or direct summer sun.

Pellet-based commercial rabbit feed provides a balanced diet. Supplement with hay at all times: rabbits need fibre for gut motility. Fresh water must be available continuously. A doe at peak lactation drinks two to three times her maintenance intake. Check drinkers twice a day in summer; in winter, check for freezing.
Place the nest box in the cage seven to ten days before the doe is due. The doe will line it with hay and fur pulled from her dewlap.

are born helpless and depend entirely on the nest for warmth. Check the nest once at 24 hours: remove any dead kits, check all remaining kits have round, full bellies, and replace the nest cover of fur. A doe that abandons the nest is usually disturbed by noise or predator presence. Keep the area quiet for the first week.

attrs.body: If one doe produces more than eight kits and another has a smaller litter, kits can be fostered within the first 24 hours. Rub the fostered kits with bedding from the receiving doe's nest before placing them in. Does recognise kits by smell, and borrowed bedding reduces rejection. Foster only when both litters are the same age within 48 hours.
